title: Misc. bug: Model weight gets evicted from idle Intel dGPU memory causing perf drop on Vulkan
author: rillomas
body:
### Name and Version λ ggml\llama-b9993-bin-win-vulkan-x64\llama-server.exe --version version: 9993 (2969d6d15) built with Clang 20.1.8 for Windows x86_64 ### Operating systems Windows ### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected? Other (Please specify in the next section) ### Command line ```shell llama-server.exe --device Vulkan0,Vulkan1 --split-mode layer --tensor-split 1,1 -m Qwen3.6-27B-UD-Q8_K_XL.gguf ``` ### Problem description & steps to reproduce Originally from #25214. After about 70 seconds of idle time, the secondary (no display connected) dGPU's dedicated memory usage starts to drop while the primary dGPU dedicated memory usage show no change. #### Primary dGPU after 70 sec idle #### Secondary dGPU after 70 sec idle After this happens, a subsequent infer request requires the weights to be copied over to VRAM again causing a significant performance drop in Prompt eval time (174.41 vs 1.93 tps). [According to community](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/25214#issuecomment-4912923058) this does not happen when NVIDIA GPU is secondary so seems like an Intel driver issue. > Moving things around so the B70 is the Primary and running the 5070 as secondary > ... > Running the b9911 release of Vulcan does not reproduce the same problem model is still loaded in to VRam after 10min of idle. #### Performance while all weight is on VRAM ``` 41.59.742.869 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 578 | prompt eval time = 1479.32 ms / 258 tokens ( 5.73 ms per token, 174.41 tokens per second) 41.59.742.879 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 578 | eval time = 28077.80 ms / 239 tokens ( 117.48 ms per token, 8.51 tokens per second) 41.59.742.880 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 578 | total time = 29557.12 ms / 497 tokens ``` #### Performance of first inference after idle ``` 41.20.870.827 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 336 | prompt eval time = 14020.46 ms / 27 tokens ( 519.28 ms per token, 1.93 tokens per second) 41.20.870.836 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 336 | eval time = 26093.99 ms / 239 tokens ( 109.18 ms per token, 9.16 tokens per second) 41.20.870.837 I slot print_timing: id 2 | task 336 | total time = 40114.45 ms / 266 tokens ``` #### Weights being copied over again after idle #### Environment - Xeon w9-3595X, Intel Arc Pro B70 x 2, GPU driver: 32.0.101.8804, Win 11 24H2. ### First Bad Commit Unknown ### Relevant log output

title: Eval bug: -ot and -ts/dev_layer() placement disagreement crashes RPC decode
author: ldumancas
body:
### Name and Version

```
$./llama-cli --version
version: 9949 (049326a00)
built with GNU 14.2.0 for Linux x86_64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

RPC, CPU

### Hardware

2-node cluster, each node is a Proxmox LXC container on a Geekom A6 (AMD Ryzen 7 6800H, 32 GB DDR5-4800). CPU-only, no GPU. Coordinator container capped at 27648 MiB/14 cores, worker container capped at 18432 MiB/10 cores (Linux memory cgroup + cpuset enforced by host). Nodes connected over LAN; the coordinator's own layer share is routed through a loopback `ggml-rpc-server` on 127.0.0.1, the worker takes a real network hop.

### Models

Qwen3.6-35B-A3B - https://huggingface.co/unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-GGUF/blob/main/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-Q4_K_M.gguf

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

Launching llama.cpp with Qwen3.6-35B-A3B across a 2-node dual-RPC topology with a `-ts` value that doesn't match the real `-ot` split ratio crashes during the server's own internal startup warm-up decode before any request is served. 

It crashes with this RPC scheduler crash signature: `ggml-rpc.cpp:498: Remote RPC server crashed or returned malformed response` despite `sched_reserve` completing cleanly first.

Right before crashing, llama.cpp `resolve_fused_ops` logs show:
```
resolve_fused_ops: layer 21 is assigned to device RPC1 but fused Gated Delta Net (autoregressive)
is assigned to device RPC0 (usually due to missing support)
resolve_fused_ops: fused Gated Delta Net (autoregressive) not supported, set to disabled
resolve_fused_ops: layer 21 is assigned to device RPC1 but fused Gated Delta Net (chunked)
is assigned to device RPC0 (usually due to missing support)
resolve_fused_ops: fused Gated Delta Net (chunked) not supported, set to disabled
```
but I believe that the message is misleading. `-ot`'s real weight placement disagrees with `dev_layer()`'s automatic placement. `-ot` sets a hard boundary at layer 25. `dev_layer()`, driven here by `-ts 1,1`, computes its own boundary near layer 20. For layers 20 through 24, the two systems disagree about which machine owns them.

I added some logging right after `resolve_fused_ops` warnings and the RPC graph scheduler logged 65  `[add_tensor] CROSS-ENDPOINT MISMATCH` errors on layer 21's tensors and Gated-Delta-Net intermediate compute nodes. Each reported a tensor reached via one RPC socket but required on another.

Commands for crashing state
```
# worker
./ggml-rpc-server -H <worker-ip> -p 50052 -t 8
# coordinator's own loopback device
./ggml-rpc-server -H 127.0.0.1 -p 50052 -t 6
# coordinator - crashes
GGML_RPC_DEBUG=1 ./llama-server \
  -m Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-Q4_K_M.gguf \
  --rpc 127.0.0.1:50052,<worker-ip>:50052 \
  -ngl 99 -ts 1,1 \
  -ot "blk\.([0-9]|1[0-9]|2[0-4])\..*=RPC0[127.0.0.1:50052],blk\.(2[5-9]|3[0-9])\..*=RPC0[<worker-ip>:50052]" \
  -c 262144 -t 2 -np 1 -fit off --no-warmup -lv 4
```
Commands for working state

```
# same as above except for -ts 3,2 instead of -ts 1,1
# worker
./ggml-rpc-server -H <worker-ip> -p 50052 -t 8
# coordinator's own loopback device
./ggml-rpc-server -H 127.0.0.1 -p 50052 -t 6
# coordinator - works
GGML_RPC_DEBUG=1 ./llama-server \
  -m Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-Q4_K_M.gguf \
  --rpc 127.0.0.1:50052,<worker-ip>:50052 \
  -ngl 99 -ts 3,2 \
  -ot "blk\.([0-9]|1[0-9]|2[0-4])\..*=RPC0[127.0.0.1:50052],blk\.(2[5-9]|3[0-9])\..*=RPC0[<worker-ip>:50052]" \
  -c 262144 -t 2 -np 1 -fit off --no-warmup -lv 4
```

I believe that my findings are that `-ot` and `-ts`/`dev_layer()` have an undocumented dependency that needs to be respected. 

`-ot`'s help text: `--override-tensor <tensor name pattern>=<buffer type>,...   override tensor buffer type` 
`-ts`'s help text: `--tensor-split N0,N1,N2,...   fraction of the model to offload to each GPU, comma-separated list of proportions, e.g. 3,1` 

There is nothing that documents the coupling of `-ot` and `-ts` and I couldn't find anything  that stated `-ot` only touches weights while another independent mechanism governs KV-cache and fused-op placement. Additionally `-ts`'s help text doesn't mention RPC devices at all even though it drives RPC device placement.

This looks like the same underlying mechanism as #24492, fixed in #25232. MTP draft/target KV-cache sharing also hits the `model.dev_layer(il)` disagreement but through a different trigger. Root cause from the PR: "the shared tensor's buffer type and the layer's buffer type differ, and the scheduler later fails to place the tensor and aborts."

Given the coupling, I would expect a startup error or logging to indicate the misconfiguration. Maybe `-ot`  and `dev_layer()` need some sort of cross validation? Is `-ot`'s weight-only scope intentional or an oversight?

### First Bad Commit

Not a regression. `-ot`/`--override-tensor` was added in #11397 as an override to weight-tensor buffer selection only. The automatic `dev_layer`/`get_layer_buft_list` table it disagrees with was introduced in #10026 and was untouched by the same commit. The check that surfaces the warning, `resolve_fused_ops` is much newer, #24646.

### Relevant log output

<details>
<summary>Logs</summary>
<!-- Copy-pasted short logs go into the "console" area here -->

```console

load_tensors:   CPU_Mapped model buffer size =   515.31 MiB
load_tensors: RPC0[127.0.0.1:50052] model buffer size = 12553.92 MiB

llama_memory_recurrent: RPC0[192.168.1.127:50052] RS buffer size =    23.03 MiB
resolve_fused_ops: Flash Attention enabled

resolve_fused_ops: fused Gated Delta Net (autoregressive) enabled

# B. Broken config (-ts 1,1 against the unchanged -ot)
# Weight placement is identical to the good config (confirms -ot alone is unaffected by -ts):
load_tensors:   CPU_Mapped model buffer size =   515.31 MiB
load_tensors: RPC0[127.0.0.1:50052] model buffer size = 12553.92 MiB

# KV-cache and recurrent-state (SSM) buffers split evenly instead of 3:2 — confirms -ts is what dev_layer() actually consults:
llama_kv_cache: RPC0[127.0.0.1:50052] KV buffer size =  2560.00 MiB

# Crash

title: Eval bug: DeepSeekV4 Flash crashes over RPC.
author: matt23654
body:
### Name and Version

build/bin/llama-cli --version
version: 9992 (6eddde06a)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64


### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

RPC

### Hardware

Gaming Desktop w/ 5090 + 64GB RAM
Strix Halo 128GB.

Gaming Desktop is CUDA with CPU offloading using the Strix Halo as a RPC server. Strix Halo tested with both Vulkan and ROCm.

### Models

bartowski/DeepSeek-V4-Flash-GGUF

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

build/bin/llama serve -hf bartowski/DeepSeek-V4-Flash-GGUF --host 127.0.0.1 --port 5000 --temp 1.0 --top-p 1.0 --min-p 0 -ngl 99 --verbose --fit off -c 60000 -np 1 -ctxcp 0 -ts 100,60 --rpc 192.168.50.90:50052 -fa off -ot "\.(3[0-9])\.ffn_.*_exps=CPU"

build_vlk/bin/ggml-rpc-server -c -H 0.0.0.0 -p 50052

This crashes with a not implemented assert for memset. I believe this is due to ``ggml_backend_tensor_memset`` not being implemented for the RPC backend. Same behavior with fa on or off. This command works correctly before #25521 .

### First Bad Commit

13f2b28b0

title: Eval bug: MMVQ host/device nwarps mismatch causes NaN on gfx11-generic / RDNA3.5
author: talhaHavadar
body:
### Name and Version

```
$ llama-cli --version
version: 9721 (dfsg)
built with gcc for x86_64-linux-gnu
```

(Ubuntu 26.10 `llama.cpp 9721+dfsg-1ubuntu1` package, `libggml0-backend-hip 0.15.2-1` from the `ppa:bullwinkle-team/rocm-with-generics` PPA, ROCm 7.2.4.)

I have also confirmed the underlying pattern still exists on `master` (`ggml/src/ggml-cuda/mmvq.cu` — host `get_device_table_id(int cc)` at ~line 89 and device `get_device_table_id()` at ~line 73 use two different lookups). See "Why #24129 doesn't fully close this" below.

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

HIP

### Hardware

AMD Ryzen AI 7 350 w/ Radeon 860M (gfx1152, RDNA3.5, Strix Halo family iGPU), integrated 16 GiB VRAM (UMA).

### Models

`unsloth/Qwen3-0.6B-GGUF:Q4_0` (any Q4_0/Q4_1/Q5_0/Q5_1/Q8_0/Q6_K/IQ4_NL model that dispatches through the MMVQ `n=1` path reproduces).

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

MMVQ produces `NaN` outputs for `ncols_dst == 1` (batch=1 decode) whenever the loaded HIP code object was compiled for `--offload-arch=gfx11-generic` and the runtime device is RDNA3.5 (gfx1150/gfx1152/gfx1153 without native code in the fatbin). Symptom in `llama-cli` is coherent-looking prompt processing followed by tokens rendered as `?????...` at plausible generation rates. HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=11.0.0 (native gfx1100) or =11.5.1 (native gfx1151) both hide the bug — they steer HIP to a native code object instead of `gfx11-generic`.

**Minimal repro** (fatbin without a gfx1152 native code object; equivalent to the current Debian/Ubuntu `ggml` build):

```
lxc launch --ephemeral --profile rocm-gpu ubuntu-daily:stonking generics-repro
lxc exec generics-repro -- sudo add-apt-repository ppa:bullwinkle-team/rocm-with-generics -y
lxc exec generics-repro -- sudo apt install -y llama.cpp libggml0-backend-hip

# NaN outputs from MMVQ (all n=1 cases fail):
lxc exec generics-repro -- /usr/libexec/x86_64-linux-gnu/ggml/test-backend-ops test -b ROCm0 -o MUL_MAT -p 'type_a=q4_0'
# ...
# [MUL_MAT] NaN at index 0 (ROCm0=nan CPU=4.080024)   MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0,type_b=f32,m=16,n=1,k=256,...): FAIL
# every n=1 case fails; every n>=2 case passes

# Garbage tokens from llama-cli:
lxc exec generics-repro -- bash -c "echo 'What is 2+2?' | llama-cli -hf unsloth/Qwen3-0.6B-GGUF:Q4_0 --predict 15 --single-turn"
# > What is 2+2?
# | ???????????????
```

`AMD_LOG_LEVEL=3` on the failing run shows the runtime resolving to the generic code object:

```
Using generic code object for device: amdgcn-amd-amdhsa--gfx1152 co: amdgcn-amd-amdhsa--gfx11-generic
```

### Root cause

`ggml/src/ggml-cuda/mmvq.cu` has two `get_device_table_id()`s and they disagree on `gfx11-generic` + RDNA3.5:

- **Device** (`__device__`, ~line 73), compile-time preprocessor: for `gfx11-generic`, `__GFX11__` is set (so `RDNA3` is defined) but none of `__gfx1150/51/52/53__` are set (so `RDNA3_5` stays undefined) → `RDNA3_0` is defined → returns `MMVQ_PARAMETERS_RDNA3_0`. For Q4_0/Q4_1/Q5_0/Q5_1/Q8_0/IQ4_NL at `ncols_dst == 1`, `calc_nwarps(...)` returns 8 (2 for Q6_K). The kernel is emitted with `__launch_bounds__(8*warp_size, 1)` and sizes `__shared__ float tmp_shared[nwarps-1][...][warp_size]` for an 8-way inter-warp reduction.
- **Host** (`__host__`, ~line 89), runtime `cc`: on gfx1152 `cc = GGML_CUDA_CC_OFFSET_AMD + 0x1152`, so `GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_RDNA3_5(cc)` is true → returns `MMVQ_PARAMETERS_RDNA2` → `calc_nwarps(...)` returns 1 → launches `block_dims = (warp_size, 1, 1)`.

Only warp 0 executes, so the `if (threadIdx.y > 0) tmp_shared[threadIdx.y-1][...] = ...` writes never happen. The reduction loop `for (int l = 0; l < nwarps-1; ++l) tmp[j][i] += tmp_shared[l][...];` runs with `nwarps == 8` (compile-time), reading uninitialized LDS for `l = 0..6` → NaN, then propagates through the rest of the graph and shows up as `?????` at the sampler.

Confirmed by the test-backend-ops bisection: every `MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0, n=1, ...)` case fails with `NaN at index 0`; every `n>=2` case passes. That's exactly the `if (ncols_dst == 1)` boundary in `calc_nwarps` where the RDNA3_0 branch would return 8 vs. falling through to 1.

Trivial FP32 kernels compiled the same way (`--offload-arch=gfx11-generic`) and run on the same gfx1152 produce correct results, so this is not a general clang miscompile of the generic target — it's specifically a host/device disagreement on the MMVQ tuning table.

### Why PR #24129 doesn't fully close this

[#24129](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/24129) (merged) added `__gfx1152__` / `__gfx1153__` to the `RDNA3_5` macro in `hip.h`. That is the right fix for the case where the fatbin **does** ship a native gfx1152 code object — the runtime loads native gfx1152, the device compile sees `__gfx1152__` → `RDNA3_5` → returns `MMVQ_PARAMETERS_RDNA2`, matches the host. Great.

But if a build has `--offload-arch=gfx11-generic` in its target list without a native gfx1152, the runtime falls through to the generic code object, and `__gfx11_generic__` (which *is* set for the generic target — confirmed with `hipcc --offload-arch=gfx11-generic -E -dM`) is not part of the `RDNA3_5` macro. So on that same hardware the mismatch is unchanged. This is the situation in the Debian/Ubuntu `ggml` build (whose target list, via `rocm-target-arch` from `pkg-rocm-tools`, currently emits `gfx11-generic gfx1100 gfx1101 gfx1151 …` without gfx1152/1153) and, from what I can tell, in the TheRock build path that [#25436](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues/25436) (DeepSeek V4 garbled on Strix Halo, still open) and possibly [#24962](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues/24962) are hitting.

More generally, the host/device pattern is inherently fragile: any future target/generic pair where the two lookups disagree will silently reintroduce this. The specific instance was fixed but the root shape wasn't.

### Proposed fix

Derive `nwarps` from the compiled kernel instead of recomputing it on the host: refactor `calc_launch_params` to be templated on `<type, c_ncols_dst, small_k>` and query the exact kernel instantiation via `cudaFuncGetAttributes` / `hipFuncGetAttributes`, deriving `nwarps = attr.maxThreadsPerBlock / warp_size` from the compiled `__launch_bounds__`. The host then always matches whatever the loaded code object declared, no matter which target/generic pair HIP loaded — robust to any current or future fatbin combination, and to the reverse case where an RDNA3.0 device happens to fall through to a `gfx11-generic` code object.

Adds two mappings to `src/ggml-cuda/vendors/hip.h` (`cudaFuncGetAttributes → hipFuncGetAttributes`, `cudaFuncAttributes → hipFuncAttributes`) since the existing HIP compat layer doesn't wrap those. Patch is at (gist link below), and I have it applied on top of the Ubuntu 0.15.2-1 tarball with a rebuild.

### Verification (post-fix, same gfx1152 hardware, no HSA override)

- `test-backend-ops test -b ROCm0 -o MUL_MAT` for every quant type in the RDNA3_0 tuning table: q4_0 (43/43), q4_1 (43/43), q5_0 (12/12), q5_1 (12/12), q8_0 (44/44), q6_K (11/11), iq4_nl (12/12).
- `llama-cli -hf unsloth/Qwen3-0.6B-GGUF:Q4_0`: coherent Qwen3 thinking output, 67 t/s generation.

### Questions

1. Is deriving `nwarps` from `cudaFuncGetAttributes` the right shape for upstream, or would you prefer a different mechanism (a runtime probe cached at init, a shared `__constant__` table_id, etc.)?
2. **CUDA side**: does the same host/device pattern exist for NVIDIA — `get_device_table_id(int cc)` on the host vs. `__CUDA_ARCH__`-driven macros on the device (`MMVQ_PARAMETERS_TURING` etc.)? I don't have hardware to test with a `--gpu-architecture=compute_70` (Volta virtual) code object running on `sm_80` (Ampere) or similar, but the pattern reads the same way to me and I'd like a second opinion before framing this as a HIP-only fix.
3. Anything in the launch path I'd be adding measurable overhead to? `cudaFuncGetAttributes` per launch feels cheap but I haven't measured on high-throughput ops.
4. Is [#25436](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues/25436) (DeepSeek V4 garbled on Strix Halo, ROCm 7.2, TheRock nightlies) plausibly the same bug? Symptoms match. If so I'd add a note there once the direction here is settled.

Happy to open a PR once the approach is agreed.

### Relevant log output

<details>
<summary>Logs</summary>

```console
$ /usr/libexec/x86_64-linux-gnu/ggml/test-backend-ops test -b ROCm0 -o MUL_MAT -p 'type_a=q4_0'
ggml_cuda_init: found 1 ROCm devices (Total VRAM: 15429 MiB):
  Device 0: AMD Radeon 860M Graphics, gfx1152 (0x1152), VMM: no, Wave Size: 32, VRAM: 15429 MiB
[MUL_MAT] NaN at index 0 (ROCm0=nan CPU=4.080024)   MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0,type_b=f32,m=16,n=1,k=256,bs=[1,1],nr=[1,1],per=[0,1,2,3],k_v=0,o=1): FAIL
  MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0,type_b=f32,m=16,n=2,k=256,bs=[1,1],nr=[1,1],per=[0,1,2,3],k_v=0,o=1): OK
  MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0,type_b=f32,m=16,n=3,k=256,bs=[1,1],nr=[1,1],per=[0,1,2,3],k_v=0,o=1): OK
  ...
Failing tests:
  MUL_MAT(type_a=q4_0,type_b=f32,m=16,n=1,k=256, ...)   [every n=1 variant]

$ echo 'What is 2+2? Reply with only the digit.' | llama-cli -hf unsloth/Qwen3-0.6B-GGUF:Q4_0 --predict 15 --single-turn --seed 42

title: Eval bug: Speculative decoding (draft-mtp / draft-dspark): greedy output diverges from vanilla on quantized targets
author: Ankk98
body:
## Summary

Under greedy sampling (`temperature=0`, `top_k=1`), **draft-model** speculative decoding can produce different text than a non-speculative run when the **target is quantized** (e.g. Q4_K_M). The same setups match on a **bf16 target**. **Ngram** speculation on the same quantized target stays lossless even when drafts are accepted.

This contradicts the expected property that speculative decoding is lossless for greedy decode (draft proposes; target verifies).

Related: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/25173 (`draft-dspark`), but the issue is **not DSpark-only**.

## Environment (local repro)

- Hardware: AMD Strix Halo iGPU
- Backend: Vulkan (`-DGGML_VULKAN=ON`)
- Branch: PR #25173
- Others have also reported similar mismatches on **CUDA** (not re-run here)

## Expected

Greedy speculative output == greedy vanilla output for the same target weights / seed / prompt.

## Actual / evidence

Probe prompt (thinking off):

> What happens when a browser requests https://example.com? Five bullet points.

### Matrix (Vulkan)

| Setup | Spec active? | Match vanilla? |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| Qwen3-8B **Q4_K_M**, vanilla ×2 | n/a | **OK** (deterministic) |
| Qwen3-8B **Q4_K_M**, `ngram-simple` (drafted, 0 accepted) | yes | **OK** |
| Qwen3-8B **Q4_K_M**, `ngram-mod` (4/8 accepted) | yes | **OK** |
| Qwen3-8B **Q4_K_M**, `draft-dspark` n_max=7 | yes (e.g. 80/336) | **BUG** |
| Qwen3-8B **Q4_K_M**, `draft-dspark`, `-fa off` | yes | **BUG** (still) |
| Qwen3-8B **bf16**, `draft-dspark` n_max=7 (4 prompts) | yes | **OK** (4/4) |
| Gemma-4-12B Q4 + `draft-mtp` n_max=4 | yes (169/340) | **BUG** |

Example Q4 DSpark fork (both `finish=stop`):

- vanilla: `...checks if it has a cached copy of the website's SSL/TLS certificate...`
- dspark: `...checks if the system has a valid certificate for **example.com**...`

## What this is / is not

- **Is quantization-related:** yes (Q4 breaks; bf16 OK for DSpark).
- **Is not “all speculative”:** ngram paths match on the same Q4 target, including with accepts.
- **Is not DSpark/DFlash-only:** Gemma **MTP** also diverges on Q4.
- **Is not Qwen-only:** Gemma-4 + MTP also fails.
- **Is not vanilla Q4 nondeterminism:** two vanilla Q4 runs match.
- **Is not FA-only:** DSpark still mismatches with `-fa off`.
- **Target weights likely fine:** ngram uses the same greedy accept/verify sampler path (`common_sampler_sample_and_accept_n` in server) and stays lossless → points to **draft-model speculative paths** (MTP / DSpark: draft ctx, KV, embd/injection, multi-token target batching) interacting badly with **quantized** target compute—not permanent weight corruption.
- **Type-cast root cause:** not isolated; path-dependent Q4 numerics remain a plausible contributor.



### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

Vulkan

### Hardware

```
ankk98@ankk98-workstation:~/repos/llama.cpp$ fastfetch

### Models

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

## Repro

```bash
# Build with Vulkan (or CUDA)
cmake -B build -DGGML_VULKAN=ON && cmake --build build -j --config Release

# Minimal probe script used locally:
# https://gist.github.com/Ankk98/ee29401e4dcdcae575e649c4a0b75d9e

  --spec-type draft-dspark --spec-draft-n-max 7 \
  -c 4096 -np 1 -ngl 99 -fa on --jinja --port 8088

# Compare greedy /v1/chat/completions (temp=0, top_k=1, enable_thinking=false)

title: Misc. bug: validation errors on /v1/chat/completions with X-Conversation-Id return 500 bad_function_call instead of 400
author: Eresy
body:
## Summary

When a `POST /v1/chat/completions` request carries the `X-Conversation-Id` header (the resumable-stream opt-in from README-dev.md) and the request body fails parameter validation, the server answers `500` with the plain-text body `bad_function_call` instead of the normal `400` JSON error. The same invalid body without the header returns the correct 400.

This hits every user of the bundled webui with a fresh profile, because the webui's default settings send `"backend_sampling": ""` (empty string, invalid type for a boolean field) on every completion, and the webui always sends `X-Conversation-Id`. Result: a first-time or settings-reset webui fails every chat with a generic "Server error (500)" modal.

## Environment

- build: b9978 (0c4fa7a989), Linux x86_64, HIP backend (gfx1151). The bug is in request handling, not in any backend.
- model: reproduced with a Gemma 4 26B-A4B gguf; the request fails before inference, so any model reproduces it.

## Reproduction

Start any `llama-server`, then:

```bash
# without the header: correct 400
curl -sw '\n%{http_code}\n' http://127.0.0.1:8081/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"m"}],"backend_sampling":""}'
# {"error":{"code":400,"message":"Field 'backend_sampling': [json.exception.type_error.302] type must be boolean, but is string","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
# 400

# with the header: 500 bad_function_call
curl -sw '\n%{http_code}\n' http://127.0.0.1:8081/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'X-Conversation-Id: repro' \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"m"}],"backend_sampling":""}'
# bad_function_call
# 500
```

Server log:

```
E srv    operator(): got exception: bad_function_call
```

Any validation failure triggers it; `backend_sampling` as a string is just the one the webui sends.

## Root cause

In `tools/server/server-stream.cpp`:

1. `server_res_spipe::set_req()` attaches the stream pipe (`spipe`) as soon as the header is present, before the request body is parsed.
2. Parameter validation throws, so `server_res_spipe::set_next()` is never called and `next_orig` stays an empty `std::function`.
3. The error path still runs `on_complete()`. Its guard checks `!spipe || next_finished`; both are false, so it calls `next_orig(chunk)` on the empty function, which throws `std::bad_function_call`.
4. The `set_exception_handler` fallback in `tools/server/server-http.cpp` converts that into the plain-text 500, replacing the 400 JSON body that was already prepared.

## Suggested fix (tested)

Extend the guard in `server_res_spipe::on_complete()`:

```cpp
if (!spipe || next_finished || !next_orig) {
    return;
}
```

Rebuilt b9978 with this guard: the repro request now returns the same 400 JSON as the header-less variant, and normal streamed chats with the header keep working. The session created by `create_or_replace()` in `set_req()` may also want eviction on this path, otherwise the failed request leaves a stream session behind.

## Related webui defect

The invalid field comes from the webui itself. In `tools/ui/src/lib/stores/chat.svelte.ts` (`apiOptions` assembly, around line 2392), every sampler field goes through a `hasValue(...)` guard that filters the empty-string placeholder used for unset settings, except this one:

```ts
apiOptions.backend_sampling = currentConfig.backend_sampling;
```

On a fresh profile `currentConfig.backend_sampling` is the `''` placeholder, so the string is sent verbatim and every completion from a default-settings webui fails. Toggling that one switch in the webui settings stores a real boolean and the failure disappears (no other setting is affected; the rest go through the guard), which made this look tied to unrelated settings changes (we first suspected the MCP opt-in). With the server-side 500 fixed the webui at least shows the real 400 message, but the field needs the same guard as its neighbors.

---

Investigated and written by the reporter with AI assistance (Claude); the analysis, reproduction, and text were reviewed and approved by a human before filing.

title: Misc. bug: split-mode tensor/row broken for mixed CUDA+ROCm setup
author: ckorfmann
body:
### Name and Version

version: 9890 (74976e1ae)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
/usr/local/bin/llama-server --split-mode tensor --model /home/myuser/models/unsloth/qwen3.6/Qwen3.6-27B-UD-Q6_K_XL.gguf --alias Qwen3.6-27B-UD-Q6_K_XL --temperature 1.0 --top-k 20 --top-p 0.95 --min-p 0.0 --presence-penalty 1.5 --ctx-size 122880 --n-gpu-layers all --device CUDA0,ROCm0 --tensor-split 1.2,1 --main-gpu 0 --cache-type-k q8_0 --cache-type-v q5_1 --spec-type draft-mtp,ngram-mod --device-draft CUDA0 --n-gpu-layers-draft all --spec-draft-n-max 3 --prio-draft 3 --no-mmproj-auto --no-mmproj-offload --mlock --no-mmap --prio 3 --cache-ram -1 --kv-unified --log-verbosity 4 --parallel 2
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

#### Hardware

- Intel i9-11900K
- NVIDIA RTX A4000
- AMD RX 6900XT

#### GGML Backends

- CUDA
- ROCm/HIP

#### Problem

`--split-mode tensor` doesn't work with my CUDA+ROCm mix (raises `GGML_ASSERT(meta_buf_ctx->bufs[i])`). Prior to [`b9890`](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases/tag/b9890), I used `-sm row` (#24216), which worked and, as expected, noticeably sped up pp/tg. Since the CUDA backend no longer supports `-sm row` (`b9890` and later), I am forced to revert back to the default `-sm layer` (which works but is slower). When using `-sm row`, I did encounter the occasional error (usually a buffer or KV cache issue) when tuning `tensor-split`, `main-gpu`, and MTP draft params, but it otherwise worked fine when properly tuned.

I have tested and `-sm tensor` and `-sm row` for the following models in `b9890` (logs for both are below), yielding identical `GGML_ASSERT(meta_buf_ctx->bufs[i])` failures across all `-sm tensor`, and identical `llama_model_load: error loading model: device CUDA0 does not support split buffers` failures across all `-sm row`:

- Qwen3.6 27B [Unsloth UD-Q5_K_XL, Unsloth UD-Q6_K_XL]
- Qwen3.6 35B-A3B [Unsloth UD-Q4_K_XL]
- Gemma4 31B [Unsloth it QAT UD-Q4_K_XL] + MTP [Unsloth it Q4_0]
- MiniMax M2.7 [Unsloth UD-IQ4_XS]

I have also tested the following, yielding the same failures:

- `main-gpu 0` vs `main-gpu 1`
- various `tensor-split`
- various `cache-ram`
- various `ctx-size`
- various `ctk` and `ctv` (symmetric and asymmetric)
- no spec/draft, `spec-type draft-mtp`, `spec-type draft-mtp,ngram-mod`

I use the CUDA and ROCm backends. I previously used CUDA+Vulkan for either and/or both GPUs in earlier builds (~`b9770` and earlier), but I had similar/identical failures with `-sm tensor`, and `-sm row` was somewhat unpredictable (leading me to switch to CUDA+ROCm, which was more stable).

#### Reproduce

I used the above `llama-server` command line for Unsloth's Qwen3.6 27B UD-Q6_K_XL. I also substituted `--split-mode tensor` for `--split-mode row`, which predictably failed.

The only set environment variable is `LLAMA_API_KEY`. I don't set `GGML_CUDA_ENABLE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1` as it markedly handicaps speed.

Here are my build commands:
```
cmake --fresh -B 'build' \
  -DLLAMA_OPENSSL=ON \
  -DGGML_BACKEND_DL=ON \
  -DGGML_NATIVE=OFF \
  -DGGML_CPU_ALL_VARIANTS=ON \
  -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
  -DGGML_BLAS=OFF \
  -DGGML_HIP=ON \

title: Eval bug: SM_60 Quality Loss, FP32 math silently done in FP16, fix merged in two forks.
author: apollo-mg
body:
### Name and Version

version: 9967 (4f37f5197)

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

CUDA

### Hardware

Linux / CUDA / 4× Tesla P100-PCIE-16GB (sm_60), dual Xeon, driver 580.159.03, CUDA 12.4. Tested on Ubuntu Server 26.04.

### Models

Qwen3.6-27B Q6_K (22,523,238,624 bytes) for the quality panel; Qwen3.6-4B dense and Darwin-36B-A3B (Qwen3.5-MoE arch) Q6_K for the speed legs.

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

SM_60 currently gets put into a hardware accelerated fast-path by default, which uses a FP16 hardware accelerator. The problem is that some FP32 math is silently getting truncated to FP16 that shouldn't be, causing significant accuracy deviations from baseline (0.002298 median KL divergence vs FP32 reference/1-in-29 flipped top tokens).

The fix for this is to carve out SM_60 the same way SM_61 already is, bypassing the Fast Path gate, and keeping FP32 math at 32 bits as it should be.

## How to Fix

261- #if defined(FP16_AVAILABLE) && __CUDA_ARCH__ != 610
261+ #if defined(FP16_AVAILABLE) && __CUDA_ARCH__ != 610 && __CUDA_ARCH__ != 600

305-        (GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_NVIDIA(cc) && fp16_available(cc) && ggml_cuda_highest_compiled_arch(cc) != 610) ||
305+        (GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_NVIDIA(cc) && fp16_available(cc) && ggml_cuda_highest_compiled_arch(cc) != 610 && ggml_cuda_highest_compiled_arch(cc) != 600) ||

311-    return (GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_NVIDIA(cc) && cc >= GGML_CUDA_CC_PASCAL && cc != 610) || GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_AMD(cc) ||
311+    return (GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_NVIDIA(cc) && cc >= GGML_CUDA_CC_PASCAL && cc != 610 && cc != 600) || GGML_CUDA_CC_IS_AMD(cc) ||

TheTom (Tom Turney) has shipped: https://github.com/TheTom/llama-cpp-turboquant/releases/tag/tqp-v0.3.0 
https://github.com/TheTom/llama-cpp-turboquant/pull/212 
spiritbuun has merged: https://github.com/spiritbuun/buun-llama-cpp/pull/80


## Steps to Reproduce:

1. Build stock llama.cpp using  `-DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=60`.
2. Generate the truth base, run `llama-perplexity --kl-divergence-base out.kld` with fp32 KV, FA off,
   on a patched build (fp32 arithmetic) — this is the step everyone skips.
3. Compare the stock vs patched runs against that base with `--kl-divergence`, 2048 ctx, 32 chunks.
4. Speed: `llama-bench` pp8192/tg32 @ d8192, patched vs unpatched, same host, nothing else
   on the GPUs.



### First Bad Commit

We followed the 610-only exemptions in their present form back through the 2024-era commits dc685be46 (#7188, FP32 FA vec kernel), 9b596417a (#7527, quantized KV for FA vec), and a818f3028 (#8075, MMQ-by-default) — sm_60 has been on the fast path the whole time,  no bisect needed.

### Relevant log output

What's below: same weights, f32 KV, FA is off on both sides; the only difference is stock fp16 arithmetic vs patched fp32 arithmetic, so it separates the arithmetic cost alone.

**Cell 0 (arithmetic-only delta, fp16 math vs fp32 math):**
median KLD **0.004962**, mean 0.023987, 99.9% 4.91, max 23.7, max Δp 98.9%,
**same-top 95.002%** — the arithmetic mode alone flips 1 in 20 greedy tokens, and about 1/1000
tokens gets its distribution basically replaced. This component is ~2× the attention-path
effect and was invisible to every prior measurement. 


Accuracy:
**Cells 1–3 **

| rung | unpatched median KLD | patched median KLD | same-top: unpatched → patched |
|---|---|---|---|
| f16-faoff | 0.002298  | **0.000001** | 96.47% → **99.89%** |
| f16-faon  | 0.002127 | **0.000000** | 96.45% → **99.93%** |
| q8-faon   | 0.002164 | **0.000005** | 96.52% → **99.80%** |


Performance:
pp8192 @ d8192, tg32 @ d8192, 4× P100 layer-split, same model:

| config | patched | unpatched | delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| f16 KV prefill | 318.53 ± 0.53 t/s | 318.60 ± 0.35 t/s | **tie** |
| f16 KV tg      | 8.64 ± 0.00 t/s   | 8.51 ± 0.01 t/s   | +1.5% patched |
| q8_0 KV prefill| 312.60 ± 0.36 t/s | 312.52 ± 0.33 t/s | **tie** |
| q8_0 KV tg     | 8.46 ± 0.01 t/s   | 8.34 ± 0.01 t/s   | +1.4% patched |
Receipts: 
https://github.com/apollo-mg/Project-Apollo/tree/main/data/carveout_panel
https://github.com/apollo-mg/Project-Apollo/blob/main/data/Apollo%20Docs/Pascal_FAST_FP16_Carveout_Results.md

title: Feature Request: Add a parameter for if reasoning is switched on by default in the llama-server webui
author: allo-
body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

The llama-server web interface allows to configure reasoning effort per-chat and defaults to `off`. Some models (like Gemma 4 E4B) do not work without reasoning and start reasoning in the answer when reasoning effort is set to `off`, so one needs to use at least `low`.

It would be good to have an option that can be put in the router mode config, what reasoning effort is the default in the web interface, while still allowing the interface to override it.

I think the web interface already stores the last used mode, but this does not help if a new client connects and the user notices a seemingly broken model that doesn't put its reasoning into the thinking collapse (when reasoning is disabled, but the model reasons nevertheless).

### Possible Implementation

Add an option like `reasoning-model = true/false` that changes relevant defaults to enable reasoning so models that do not work well without reasoning are used correctly with reasoning on by default.

The API already works correctly as it uses the chat template default which is reasoning enabled. An alternative implementation would be to follow the chat template default, but I think this may not work reliably if the jinja templates of different models have notable differences in how they set the default.

title: Eval bug: deepseek4 (DeepSeek-V4-Flash): garbled/degraded output when MoE expert layers run on CUDA — CPU-only experts (--n-cpu-moe) produce correct output
author: Cybertiron
body:
### Name and Version

llama-server, version b9940 (259f2e2a5), built with Clang 20.1.8 for Windows
x86_64, CUDA backend (cublas64_13 / CUDA 13.3 runtime).

### Operating systems

Windows

### GGML backends

CUDA

### Hardware

Windows 10 Enterprise 10.0.19045; 2× RTX 3090 (24 GB each); 128 GB RAM.

### Models

UD-Q3_K_XL (~120 GB, 4-way split GGUF) 
UD-IQ1_S (~77 GB, 3-way split)

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

### What happened?

With DeepSeek-V4-Flash GGUF models, output quality degrades in direct proportion
to how many MoE expert layers are computed on CUDA GPUs. With all experts kept
on CPU (`--n-cpu-moe 999`), output is fully coherent. As `--n-cpu-moe` is
lowered (more expert layers on GPU), the model progressively degrades: it
starts answering in the wrong language (Latvian instead of the requested
Lithuanian — i.e. "almost right" tokens) and becomes increasingly incoherent.

| --n-cpu-moe (model has 43 layers) | expert layers on GPU | output |
|---|---|---|
| 64 / 999 (all experts on CPU) | 0 | correct: coherent Lithuanian, can write a poem |
| 34 | 9 | wrong language (Latvian), degraded coherence |
| 30–32 | 11–13 | worse: barely coherent, cannot complete simple creative tasks |

Key facts:
- Reproduces identically on **two different quants**: UD-Q3_K_XL (~120 GB, 4-way
  split GGUF) and UD-IQ1_S (~77 GB, 3-way split) from
  https://huggingface.co/unsloth/DeepSeek-V4-Flash-GGUF — so it is not a
  quant-specific artifact.
- Expert tensor **placement should be mathematically neutral**; only this arch
  misbehaves. On the same machine/build, `gemma4` and `qwen35moe` MoE models
  run fully GPU-offloaded with correct output, so the CUDA setup itself is fine.
- Non-expert weights (attention etc.) on GPU are fine: runs with attention on
  GPU + all experts on CPU produce correct output.
- Not the known CUDA 13.2 gibberish issue — this build links `cublas64_13.dll`
  (CUDA runtime 13.3).

### Steps to reproduce

1. Load DeepSeek-V4-Flash UD-Q3_K_XL (or UD-IQ1_S):
```
llama-server -m DeepSeek-V4-Flash-UD-Q3_K_XL-00001-of-00004.gguf ^
  --gpu-layers 99 --split-mode layer --tensor-split 6.3,1 ^
  --n-cpu-moe 34 --ctx-size 8192 --flash-attn on --jinja ^
  --temp 0.8 --top-p 0.95 --min-p 0.05
```
2. Ask for a short text in a low-resource language (e.g. Lithuanian:
   "Parašyk trumpą eilėraštį apie knygas").
3. Observe wrong-language / incoherent output.
4. Relaunch with `--n-cpu-moe 999` (all experts on CPU), same prompt →
   correct, coherent output.


### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

ggml-cuda (MoE expert `mul_mat_id` path for the `deepseek4` architecture,
possibly interacting with the new indexer attention).


### First Bad Commit

_No response_

### Relevant log output

Model loads and serves without errors — the failure is silent quality
degradation of GPU-computed expert layers, not a crash.
